severity_assessment: CRITICAL - Race condition in user creation/linking (TOCTOU)
rationale: Classic Time-of-Check-Time-of-Use race condition in callback/route.ts lines 150-169. Two concurrent OAuth callbacks for the same Google user can both pass getUserByGoogleId() as null, then both pass getUserByEmail() as null, resulting in duplicate user creation. This is both a Tampering (T-01) finding (data modification via race) and Privilege Escalation (E-01) finding (duplicate accounts enable account takeover via linking race).
additional_vectors: Request A and Request B both check getUserByGoogleId() → null within same 10ms window
Both pass getUserByEmail() → null
Both call createUserWithGoogle() → duplicate user created
Attacker can link second account to existing email account for privilege escalation
mitigation_adequacy: INADEQUATE - MOCK database has no atomicity, no unique constraints, no transactions. Production requires PostgreSQL with unique constraint on google_id or Redis distributed lock.
mitigation_gaps: No database-level unique constraint on google_id
No atomic upsert operation
No transaction wrapper around check-create sequence
No Redis distributed lock as fallback
MOCK database pattern will cause race conditions in production

strategic_trade_offs.simplicity: MOCK in-memory Map is simple but not safe for concurrent access
strategic_trade_offs.performance: PostgreSQL FOR UPDATE row lock adds latency but ensures correctness
strategic_trade_offs.complexity: Redis distributed lock adds infrastructure dependency

recommendation: IMPLEMENT IMMEDIATELY - Use PostgreSQL unique constraint on google_id with atomic upsert. Add Redis distributed lock as fallback for high availability. Replace MOCK users.ts with production PostgreSQL implementation before launch.

severity_assessment: CRITICAL - Session token transmitted as URL query parameter
rationale: callback/route.ts lines 185-191 appends session.token as a URL search parameter. This exposes the session token through multiple vectors: server access logs (token in plaintext), browser history (token stored in URL), Referer header (token leaked to external sites), browser tabs/favorites (token in saved URLs), screen sharing/screenshots.
additional_vectors: Server access logs capture token in plaintext
Browser history stores token in URL
Referer header leaks token when clicking external links
Browser tabs/favorites preserve token in saved URLs
Screen sharing exposes token in address bar
mitigation_adequacy: NOT IMPLEMENTED - Code still uses URL query parameter
mitigation_gaps: Token appears in server-side logs
Token persists in browser history
Token leaks via Referer header
No HTTP-only cookie alternative implemented
Frontend still expects token from URL parameter

strategic_trade_offs.simplicity: URL parameter is simple but insecure
strategic_trade_offs.compatibility: Cookie requires frontend update to read from cookie instead of URL
strategic_trade_offs.security: HTTP-only Secure SameSite=Strict cookie eliminates all exposure vectors

recommendation: IMPLEMENT IMMEDIATELY - Replace URL parameter with HTTP-only, Secure, SameSite=Strict cookie. Update frontend to read session_token from cookie instead of URL param. Do NOT include token in redirect URL.

severity_assessment: HIGH - No redirect URI validation against open redirect
rationale: callback/route.ts line 32 uses OAUTH_SUCCESS_REDIRECT_URL directly from environment variable without validation. If the env var is compromised or misconfigured, attackers could redirect users to a phishing site after successful OAuth authentication.
additional_vectors: Environment variable injection via supply chain attack
Misconfigured deployment pipeline
Accidental misconfiguration of OAUTH_SUCCESS_REDIRECT_URL
Phishing site receives authenticated session
mitigation_adequacy: NOT IMPLEMENTED - No validation of redirect URL
mitigation_gaps: No whitelist of allowed redirect domains
No validation that redirect is relative path or allowed domain
No check for suspicious redirect patterns (//, \, @)
No runtime validation at startup

strategic_trade_offs.usability: Flexible redirect URL enables custom success pages but introduces risk
strategic_trade_offs.security: Strict domain whitelist eliminates open redirect but limits flexibility
strategic_trade_offs.operational: Startup validation fails fast on misconfiguration

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Add ALLOWED_REDIRECT_DOMAINS env var with comma-separated whitelist. Validate redirect URL at startup: must be relative path OR match allowed domain. Fail fast on invalid configuration.

severity_assessment: HIGH - No PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange) implementation
rationale: lib/auth/oauth/google.ts lines 75-95 (getGoogleAuthUrl) does not implement PKCE. On public clients (mobile apps, SPAs), the authorization code could be intercepted via URI schemes, proxy servers, or browser history. Without PKCE, authorization code interception leads directly to session token theft.
additional_vectors: URI scheme interception on mobile apps
Proxy server logging of authorization codes
Browser history exposure of authorization URLs
Man-in-the-middle interception of authorization code
mitigation_adequacy: NOT IMPLEMENTED - No PKCE code in codebase
mitigation_gaps: No code_verifier generation (32-byte random)
No code_challenge generation (SHA256 of verifier)
No code_challenge_method=S256 parameter
No cookie storage of verifier for callback
No verification of verifier in callback

strategic_trade_offs.complexity: PKCE adds ~30 lines and cookie management
strategic_trade_offs.compatibility: Google supports PKCE, all modern OAuth clients require it
strategic_trade_offs.security: PKCE eliminates authorization code interception attack vector

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Add PKCE support: generate 32-byte random verifier, SHA256 hash as challenge, store verifier in httpOnly cookie, verify in callback. Required for public clients.

severity_assessment: MEDIUM - No CSRF protection via state/code binding
rationale: callback/route.ts lines 62-180 validates state nonce for CSRF but does not bind the authorization code to the state. An attacker with a valid state cookie could potentially pair it with a different authorization code, completing a CSRF attack where the victim's session is linked to the attacker's Google account.
additional_vectors: Attacker obtains valid state nonce
Victim initiates OAuth flow
Attacker intercepts authorization code from their own flow
Attacker provides victim's state with attacker's code
Victim's session linked to attacker's Google account
mitigation_adequacy: PARTIAL - State nonce exists but not bound to authorization code
mitigation_gaps: State nonce does not include code challenge
No binding between state and authorization code
Attacker can reuse state with different code
No verification that code was initiated with this state

strategic_trade_offs.simplicity: Standalone state nonce is simple but less secure
strategic_trade_offs.security: Code binding prevents state reuse attacks
strategic_trade_offs.complexity: Hashing code into state adds complexity but strengthens security

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Include SHA256 hash of expected authorization code in state: state = nonceBase64.codeChallenge. In callback, verify codeChallenge matches received code. Prevents state reuse with different code.

severity_assessment: HIGH - Error messages may leak internal details
rationale: callback/route.ts lines 192-205 error handler logs error.message which may contain sensitive context if the error includes request details. While IP is correctly hashed, the error message itself could expose session tokens, internal paths, or other auth context to logs.
additional_vectors: Error message contains session token from failed operation
Error message exposes internal file paths
Error message reveals Redis/database error details
Logs stored unencrypted enabling data breach exposure
mitigation_adequacy: PARTIAL - IP is hashed but error message not sanitized
mitigation_gaps: error.message not truncated
No sanitization of sensitive context in errors
Full error details logged without redaction
No separation of user-facing and server-facing errors

strategic_trade_offs.debugging: Full error details aid debugging but leak sensitive data
strategic_trade_offs.security: Truncated errors prevent leakage but reduce debuggability
strategic_trade_offs.compliance: Audit logs should not contain PII or auth secrets

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Truncate error.message to 100 chars, sanitize any request context, separate user-facing errors (generic) from server logs (detailed). Never log session tokens.

severity_assessment: HIGH - No rate limiting on OAuth endpoints (initiation + callback)
rationale: route.ts lines 47-75 (initiation) and callback/route.ts lines 62-180 (callback) have no rate limiting. An attacker could rapidly generate state nonces (D-01, exhausting Redis storage at ~100 bytes/nonce = 1GB per 10M requests) or repeatedly send callback requests with invalid codes (D-02, causing Redis ops, token exchange attempts, user lookups).
additional_vectors: State nonce flood fills Redis storage (D-01)
Invalid callback flood causes external API calls (D-02)
Redis operations per request multiply attack impact
User enumeration via repeated callback attempts
Denial of service via resource exhaustion
mitigation_adequacy: NOT IMPLEMENTED - No rate limiting code in either endpoint
mitigation_gaps: No rate limit on initiation endpoint
No rate limit on callback endpoint
No Redis-based distributed rate limiting
No tracking of attempts per IP/client
No 429 response with Retry-After header

strategic_trade_offs.simplicity: No rate limiting is simple but insecure
strategic_trade_offs.availability: Rate limiting prevents DoS but may affect legitimate users
strategic_trade_offs.accuracy: Distributed rate limiting requires Redis but works across instances

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Add rate limiting to both endpoints: 10 initiations per 15 min per IP, 30 callbacks per 15 min per IP. Use Redis-backed distributed rate limiting. Return 429 with Retry-After.

severity_assessment: MEDIUM - Cookie-based state validation lacks client binding
rationale: lib/auth/redis.ts lines 180-230 (verifySession) HMAC integrity signatures are computed and verified, but session binding validation (IP/UA) is optional and can be disabled via SESSION_BINDING_CONFIG.enabled: false. Without mandatory session binding, stolen cookies enable session hijacking without detection.
additional_vectors: Cookie stolen via XSS
Cookie intercepted via network sniffing
Cookie leaked via logs or backups
Attacker uses stolen cookie from different IP/UA
No detection of session theft
mitigation_adequacy: INADEQUATE - Session binding can be disabled via config
mitigation_gaps: SESSION_BINDING_CONFIG.enabled is configurable (should be always on)
No mandatory enforcement in production
IP subnet and UA threshold configurable (not fixed)
Optional binding defeats the security purpose

strategic_trade_offs.flexibility: Configurable binding allows testing but weakens security
strategic_trade_offs.security: Mandatory binding prevents session theft without detection
strategic_trade_offs.usability: Loose binding (24-bit subnet, 0.7 UA threshold) balances security and usability

recommendation: IMPLEMENT BEFORE PRODUCTION - Make SESSION_BINDING_CONFIG.enabled always true in production (const, not variable). Set fixed values: ipSubnetBits: 24, uaMatchThreshold: 0.7. Never allow disabling in production.
